class_schedule.topic: The Policy Landscape: Who Governs Educational Technology?
learning_objectives: Map the governance structures that shape educational technology policy
Identify federal, state, and institutional policy mechanisms
Analyze how policy lags behind technology development
readings: Reidenberg, J. et al. - Privacy and Cloud Computing in Public Schools
FERPA, COPPA, and CIPA overview documents

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational technology operates within a web of laws, regulations, and institutional policies that most technologists never read. Understanding this policy landscape is essential for making defensible decisions and avoiding legal liability.
key_concepts: Federal laws: FERPA (student records), COPPA (children's privacy), CIPA (internet filtering), Section 508 (accessibility)
State-level policies: student data privacy laws, ed tech procurement requirements
Institutional policies: acceptable use, data governance, AI use, accessibility standards
The policy-technology gap: legislation written for paper records applied to AI systems
Policy actors: legislators, school boards, unions, vendors, advocacy groups
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy mapping exercise: trace how a single technology decision (adopting a new learning app) intersects with FERPA, COPPA, state data privacy law, district acceptable use policy, and accessibility requirements

review.value: The Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act protects student education records. Any technology that stores student data must comply. The 'school official exception' allows ed tech vendors to access records only under strict conditions.
FERPA was written in 1974. COPPA in 1998. Neither anticipated AI, learning analytics, or cloud computing. Policy frameworks are being stretched to cover technologies their authors never imagined.

class_schedule.topic: Student Data Privacy in the Digital Age
learning_objectives: Evaluate the privacy implications of educational data collection and use
Design data governance frameworks for educational institutions
Apply privacy-by-design principles to educational technology
readings: Zeide, E. - The Structural Consequences of Big Data-Driven Education
Student Privacy Compass - Best Practices for Schools

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational technology collects unprecedented amounts of student data: behavioral, academic, biometric, and emotional. This data can improve learning, but it can also be misused, breached, or exploited. Data governance is a policy imperative.
key_concepts: Data types: academic records, behavioral data, biometric data, metadata
Data lifecycle: collection, storage, use, sharing, retention, deletion
Privacy by design: embed privacy protections into system architecture
Third-party data sharing: vendor contracts, FERPA compliance, data mining
Student data breaches: prevalence, consequences, and prevention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data governance audit: examine a school district's vendor contracts for data protection provisions, identifying gaps and recommending improvements

review.value: Cavoukian's seven principles: proactive not reactive, privacy as the default, privacy embedded into design, full functionality (no trade-offs), end-to-end security, visibility and transparency, respect for user privacy.

Most vendor terms of service are written by the vendor's lawyers to protect the vendor. Schools must negotiate data ownership, deletion rights, breach notification requirements, and restrictions on secondary data use.

learning_objectives: Analyze current and proposed AI governance frameworks for education
Evaluate algorithmic accountability mechanisms for educational AI
Design AI governance policies for educational institutions
readings: UNESCO - Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021)
Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights (OSTP, 2022) - education applications

lecture_notes.introduction: AI systems are making consequential decisions in education: who gets admitted, what content students see, how writing is scored, and which students are flagged as at-risk. Governance frameworks must ensure these systems are fair, transparent, and accountable.
key_concepts: AI governance principles: fairness, transparency, accountability, safety, privacy
Algorithmic impact assessment: evaluating AI systems before deployment
Explainability requirements: can the AI explain its decisions?
Bias auditing: testing AI systems for demographic fairness
Institutional AI governance: who decides which AI systems are adopted?
lecture_notes.demonstration: AI governance policy drafting: create an institutional AI governance policy that addresses procurement, deployment, monitoring, and accountability for AI systems in education
setup_requirements: Case studies of AI governance failures

review.value: Before deploying an AI system that affects students, conduct a structured assessment: What decisions does it make? What data does it use? How was it trained? Has it been tested for bias? What happens when it is wrong? Who is accountable?
If an AI system flags a student as at-risk or rejects an application, should the student be entitled to an explanation of why? Current law is unclear, but ethical practice demands transparency.

class_schedule.topic: Digital Equity Policy: Access, Participation, and Outcomes
learning_objectives: Analyze digital equity policies at federal, state, and local levels
Evaluate the effectiveness of 1:1 device programs and broadband initiatives
Design policy proposals that address all three levels of the digital divide
readings: Warschauer, M. & Tate, T. - Digital Divides and Social Inclusion
FCC - Emergency Connectivity Fund and E-Rate program documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital equity is a policy choice, not an inevitable outcome. Governments, school districts, and institutions make deliberate decisions about who gets access, what kind of access, and what support accompanies it.
key_concepts: Federal programs: E-Rate, Emergency Connectivity Fund, Affordable Connectivity Program
State digital equity plans: infrastructure, devices, training, content
1:1 device programs: implementation evidence and equity outcomes
Homework gap: students without home internet access
Policy beyond access: digital skills training, quality of use, outcome equity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy proposal exercise: draft a digital equity policy for a school district that addresses devices, connectivity, digital literacy training, and accessible content

review.value: An estimated 15-16 million K-12 students in the U.S. lack adequate home internet access. These students cannot complete online homework, access digital resources, or participate in remote learning. The homework gap is a policy failure, not an individual one.
Providing a device without training, support, and quality content is like providing a textbook in a language the student cannot read. Access is necessary but not sufficient for digital equity.

class_schedule.topic: Intellectual Property, OER, and Content Licensing
learning_objectives: Apply copyright law to educational technology content creation
Evaluate fair use doctrine in digital educational contexts
Design content licensing strategies using Creative Commons
readings: Crews, K. - Copyright Law for Librarians and Educators Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational technologists create, remix, and distribute content constantly. Understanding copyright, fair use, Creative Commons, and open licensing is essential for legal compliance and for advancing the open education movement.
key_concepts: Copyright basics: what is protected, duration, exclusive rights
Fair use in education: four factors, classroom copying guidelines, TEACH Act
Creative Commons licenses: CC BY, CC BY-SA, CC BY-NC, CC BY-NC-SA, CC0
OER and open licensing as an equity strategy
AI-generated content: who owns it?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Licensing exercise: evaluate five content scenarios (remixing a video, using an image in an online course, adapting OER, sharing student work, using AI-generated content) and determine the correct licensing approach for each

review.value: Fair use is a defense, not a right. The four factors (purpose, nature, amount, market effect) must be weighed for each use. Educational purpose helps but does not automatically qualify as fair use, especially for digital distribution.

AI-generated content occupies an uncertain legal space. The U.S. Copyright Office has ruled that purely AI-generated works are not copyrightable because they lack human authorship. Works with significant human creative input that incorporate AI may be protectable.

class_schedule.topic: Professional Ethics and the Ed Tech Practitioner
learning_objectives: Apply professional ethical frameworks to educational technology practice
Navigate ethical dilemmas involving competing stakeholder interests
Develop a personal code of professional ethics for educational technology
readings: Selwyn, N. - What's the Problem with Ed-Tech? Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational technology practitioners face ethical dilemmas that no single law or policy can resolve: when a vendor offers a free product that mines student data, when an administrator demands AI proctoring over faculty objections, when budget constraints force accessibility compromises. Your professional ethics guide these decisions.
key_concepts: AECT Code of Ethics: commitment to individual, society, and profession
Ethical reasoning frameworks: consequentialism, deontology, virtue ethics, care ethics
Common dilemmas: privacy vs. analytics, accessibility vs. budget, innovation vs. equity
Whistleblowing and professional courage: when to speak up
Building an ethical professional identity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical dilemma resolution: work through three realistic ed tech dilemmas using multiple ethical frameworks, then draft a personal code of professional ethics

review.value: Three commitments: to the individual (privacy, autonomy, growth), to society (equity, access, public good), and to the profession (competence, integrity, continuous learning). When these conflict, ethical reasoning is required.
Sometimes ethical practice requires saying no to a supervisor, a vendor, or a board. The educational technologist who understands policy, law, and ethics has the standing to push back constructively.

quizzes.topic: Policy Landscape, Privacy, and AI Governance

questions.question: A school district adopts a free educational app that requires students to create accounts with email addresses and tracks their usage behavior. The app's privacy policy states that data may be shared with 'marketing partners.' Identify at least two legal violations and explain the required corrective action.
questions.answer: Violation 1: COPPA — if students are under 13, collecting personal information (email addresses) and sharing data with marketing partners without verified parental consent violates the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act. Violation 2: FERPA — student usage data linked to identifiable accounts constitutes educational records. Sharing this data with marketing partners exceeds the 'school official exception' and violates FERPA's prohibition on unauthorized disclosure. Corrective actions: (1) Immediately cease using the app. (2) Request data deletion from the vendor. (3) Negotiate a vendor contract that complies with COPPA and FERPA, including data ownership by the district, prohibition on marketing use, and breach notification requirements. (4) Implement a vendor vetting process for all future app adoptions.

questions.question: What is an algorithmic impact assessment for educational AI, and what key questions should it answer before an AI system is deployed in schools?
questions.answer: An algorithmic impact assessment is a structured evaluation conducted before deploying an AI system that makes decisions affecting students. Key questions: (1) What decisions does the system make or inform? (e.g., flagging at-risk students, scoring essays, recommending content) (2) What data does it use, and was the training data representative of the student population? (3) Has the system been tested for bias across demographic groups (race, gender, disability, socioeconomic status)? (4) What is the error rate, and what are the consequences of errors for students? (5) Can the system explain its decisions in terms a student or parent can understand? (6) Who is accountable when the system makes an incorrect or harmful decision? (7) What is the oversight mechanism for ongoing monitoring?

rubric.key: Lists at least four relevant key questions
Questions address fairness, transparency, and accountability

quizzes.topic: Digital Equity, Copyright, and Professional Ethics

questions.question: A teacher remixes a copyrighted textbook chapter with their own commentary and a Creative Commons video, then posts the package on the public course website. Analyze the copyright issues and recommend a legal approach.
questions.answer: Issues: (1) The textbook chapter is copyrighted. Posting it on a PUBLIC website likely exceeds fair use because the fourth factor (market effect) is significantly harmed — the publisher loses potential sales when the content is freely available to anyone. Posting within a password-protected LMS would strengthen the fair use argument. (2) The Creative Commons video's specific license matters: if it is CC BY-NC, the remix is likely fine for educational non-commercial use but must include attribution. If it is CC BY-ND (no derivatives), remixing is prohibited. Legal approach: (1) Move the textbook content behind LMS authentication. (2) Use only the minimum necessary portion of the textbook (strengthens fair use factor 3). (3) Verify the CC video's license and comply with its terms. (4) Add proper attribution for the CC content. (5) Consider replacing the textbook excerpt with an OER alternative.

rubric.key: Identifies the copyright issue with the textbook chapter on a public site
Analyzes the Creative Commons license implications correctly
Recommends a legal approach addressing both issues

questions.question: An administrator asks you to implement an AI proctoring system that faculty and students have strongly opposed due to privacy and bias concerns. Using ethical reasoning, describe how you would respond.
questions.answer: Ethical response using multiple frameworks: Consequentialist: the harms (documented racial bias in AI proctoring, student anxiety, privacy invasion, damage to institutional trust) likely outweigh the benefits (reduced cheating). Deontological: continuous surveillance of students in their homes violates their dignity and autonomy as moral agents. Care ethics: the system damages the trusting relationship between institution and students. Professional ethics (AECT): commitment to the individual requires protecting student privacy and well-being. Response: (1) Present the evidence on bias and privacy concerns to the administrator professionally. (2) Propose evidence-based alternatives: authentic assessment design, oral exams, process-based assessment. (3) If overruled, document your objection in writing. (4) Advocate for transparency with students about what data is collected. (5) Push for ongoing bias auditing if the system is deployed. Sometimes ethical practice means disagreeing respectfully with authority.

rubric.key: Applies at least two ethical frameworks
Describes professional approach including escalation if needed

major_assignments.title: Educational Technology Policy Analysis and Proposal
major_assignments.description: Select a current educational technology policy issue (e.g., AI in schools, student data privacy, digital equity, content licensing, algorithmic accountability). Produce: (1) a policy analysis of the current landscape, including relevant laws, regulations, and institutional policies; (2) a stakeholder analysis identifying competing interests; (3) a policy proposal with specific recommendations; and (4) an implementation plan.
objectives: Analyze the policy landscape for a specific ed tech issue
Identify and balance competing stakeholder interests
Draft a professional policy proposal with actionable recommendations
Design an implementation plan with political feasibility assessment
deliverables: Implementation plan with timeline and political strategy

criteria: Accurate description of current legal and regulatory landscape
Identifies gaps and contradictions in current policy
Uses evidence from research and case examples
Accurately describes each group's interests and concerns
Addresses genuine conflicts, not just consensus positions
Addresses identified gaps and stakeholder concerns
Grounded in evidence and ethical principles
Rigorous policy analysis with accurate legal foundations

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on policy, ethics, and legal frameworks

participation.description: Policy debates, ethical dilemma discussions, and stakeholder simulations

major_assignments.description: Educational technology policy analysis and proposal

midterm_examination.description: Covers legal frameworks, privacy, AI governance, and digital equity

final_project.description: Personal code of professional ethics with case-based justification and peer defense

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected. Policy debates and ethical discussions require participation. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Policy analysis drafts must be submitted on time for peer review.
course_policies.collaboration: Policy debate teams and peer review are integral. All policy analyses must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citation. Legal references must be accurate. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access for legal research. Access to policy databases and legal resources.
